title: Pipeline Steps of R-CNN
concept: The R-CNN pipeline consists of four distinct steps: Region Proposal, Feature Extraction (CNN), Classification (SVMs), and Bounding Box Regression.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main role of the CNN in R-CNN?

options.text: To extract a feature vector from each cropped region proposal
To draw the final bounding boxes on the screen
To split pixels into color regions

exercises.explain: The CNN functions as a feature extractor, outputting a numeric feature vector for each crop.

cards.title: Assembly Line
cards.body: In a car factory, one robot stamps the metal, a second welds the frame, a third paints the body, and a fourth installs the engine. Each step is separate. The original R-CNN works like this: it is a pipeline of separate modules chained together.

cards.title: The Four Pipeline Steps
cards.body: The R-CNN architecture is split into 4 distinct phases: 1) Region Proposal (finding crops), 2) Feature Extraction (CNN runs), 3) Classification (linear Support Vector Machines classify the features), and 4) Bounding Box Regression (fine-tuning coordinates).

cards.title: Casting, Costume, and Acting
cards.body: First, you cast 2,000 actors (proposals). Second, you dress them in a standard uniform (warping to 224x224). Third, a panel of specialist judges grades each actor on specific skills (SVMs). Fourth, you adjust their standing positions on stage (box regression).

cards.title: Chaining the Modules
cards.body: For each of the 2,000 crops, a pre-trained CNN (like AlexNet) extracts a 4096-dimensional feature vector. This vector is saved to disk and then fed to multiple linear SVMs (one for each class) for final labeling.

cards.title: Softmax was not used at first
cards.body: You might think the CNN classified objects directly using a Softmax layer. In the original R-CNN, researchers actually replaced the network's Softmax layer with separate linear SVMs because SVMs gave slightly higher accuracy on the datasets of that era.

cards.title: R-CNN Block Diagram
cards.body: Visualizing the pipeline sequence of original R-CNN:

left.text: Selective Search proposals -> Warping -> CNN Feature extraction.

right.text: Feature vector -> SVM Classifiers -> Bounding Box Regressor.

exercises.prompt: What classifier did the original R-CNN use to label features extracted by the CNN?
exercises.explain: The original R-CNN used linear SVMs trained on extracted CNN features to perform classification.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Bounding box regression in R-CNN is used to adjust the coordinate offsets of the proposed regions.
exercises.explain: True. The final stage uses a regression model to correct minor offsets in the proposed region coordinates.
The R-CNN pipeline features four discrete modules: proposals, CNN features, SVMs, and regression.
exercises.sentence: The original R-CNN pipeline contains ___ distinct steps that must be trained separately.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a step in the R-CNN pipeline?
exercises.explain: The Region Proposal Network (RPN) was introduced later in Faster R-CNN, not the original R-CNN.

options.text: A Region Proposal Network (RPN) predicting anchors
Selective Search generating regions of interest

exercises.prompt: You are building a pipeline using the original R-CNN framework. Which of the following is a major training headache of this architecture?

options.text: You must train and store features for the CNN, SVMs, and regressors separately, which takes massive disk space and time.
It requires all training images to be in grayscale.
It can only train on images that have exactly one object.
You cannot run the CNN on a GPU during training.

exercises.explain: Exactly! R-CNN's multi-stage pipeline is not trained jointly (end-to-end), requiring separate training runs for each module and massive disk storage for extracted features. Next: let's learn how R-CNN feeds rectangular regions into square networks!
